questions.question: What is the mathematical condition for equilibrium?
questions.explanation: Equilibrium occurs when the net force equals zero: $F_{net} = 0$, resulting in zero acceleration according to Newton's Second Law.

questions.question: What is the difference between static and dynamic equilibrium?
options: Static has constant velocity, dynamic has acceleration
Static is at rest, dynamic has constant velocity
Static has net force, dynamic has zero net force
Static involves rotation, dynamic involves translation
questions.explanation: Static equilibrium: object at rest ($v = 0$). Dynamic equilibrium: object moving with constant velocity ($v = \text{constant}$). Both have $F_{net} = 0$.

questions.question: A book rests on a table. What forces are balanced in vertical equilibrium?
options: Weight down and normal force up
Normal force up and applied force sideways
questions.explanation: In vertical equilibrium, the weight downward is balanced by the normal force upward from the table: $F_g = F_N$.

questions.question: What condition must be satisfied for horizontal equilibrium?
questions.explanation: Horizontal equilibrium requires the sum of all horizontal force components to equal zero: $\Sigma F_x = 0$.

questions.question: A car moves at constant speed on a straight highway. Why is this dynamic equilibrium?
options: The engine force is greater than friction
questions.explanation: At constant velocity, the engine force forward exactly balances friction and air resistance backward, so $F_{net} = 0$ - this is dynamic equilibrium.

questions.question: What is the weight of a 5 kg object on Earth? (g = 9.8 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Weight W = mg = 5 × 9.8 = 49 N.

questions.question: A sign hangs motionless from two cables. What equilibrium conditions apply?
questions.explanation: The sign experiences both vertical equilibrium (weight balanced by vertical tension components) and horizontal equilibrium (horizontal tension components cancel).

questions.question: James and Charles are pulling a long rope at either end. What force is applied to the rope?
questions.explanation: When two people pull on opposite ends of a rope, the rope experiences tension — a pulling force that acts along its length.

questions.question: Which of the following is a disadvantage of friction?
options: Makes it more difficult to walk
questions.explanation: Friction causes wear and tear on machine parts over time. The other options are incorrect — friction actually helps prevent skidding and makes walking possible.

questions.question: Which of the following is the exact synonym of kinetic friction?
questions.explanation: "Kinetic friction" and "dynamic friction" are interchangeable terms for the friction that opposes motion between surfaces already sliding past each other. Rolling and sliding friction are specific types, not synonyms.

questions.question: Which of the following has the highest coefficient of friction for the same surfaces?
questions.explanation: The coefficient of static friction (μ_s) is always greater than kinetic/sliding friction (μ_k) for the same surfaces. Rolling friction is typically the lowest of all.

questions.question: What happens to an object in equilibrium if one force suddenly increases?
options: It accelerates in the direction of the increased force
questions.explanation: If one force increases, the net force is no longer zero, and the object accelerates according to $F_{net} = ma$ in the direction of the net force.

questions.question: Which of the following is a contact force?
questions.explanation: Friction requires surfaces to be in contact. Gravity, magnetic, and electrostatic forces act at a distance.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a free-body diagram in equilibrium analysis?
options: To identify all forces acting on the object
questions.explanation: Free-body diagrams show all external forces acting on an object, which is essential for applying equilibrium conditions $\Sigma F_x = 0$ and $\Sigma F_y = 0$.

questions.question: A 50 N object hangs from a single vertical rope. What is the tension in the rope?
questions.explanation: In vertical equilibrium, tension upward equals weight downward: $T = F_g = 50$ N.

questions.question: When is the force in a rope supporting a hanging object equal to its weight? (Select all that apply)
options: When the object is at rest
When the object is moving at constant velocity
When the object is accelerating upward
When the object is accelerating downward
questions.explanation: The force in a rope (tension) equals weight (T = mg) whenever acceleration is zero — whether the object is at rest or moving at constant velocity. If accelerating upward, T > mg; if accelerating downward, T < mg.

questions.question: A box is pushed across a floor at constant velocity. What is true about the forces acting on it?
options: The applied force is greater than friction
No forces act on the box
Only gravity acts on the box
questions.explanation: Constant velocity means zero net force, so the applied force and friction are balanced.

questions.question: A box slides at constant speed across a rough floor. What force balances the applied push?
questions.explanation: At constant speed, the applied push forward is balanced by kinetic friction backward, maintaining horizontal equilibrium.

questions.question: Which of these show that an object is at rest?
questions.explanation: An object at rest has no change in position (Δx = 0) and balanced forces acting on it (ΣF = 0).

questions.question: A toy car experiences a propulsive force of 0.8 N and accelerates at 4 m/s². What is its mass in grams?
questions.explanation: m = F/a = 0.8/4 = 0.2 kg. Converting to grams: 0.2 kg × 1000 = 200 g.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered equilibrium concepts and applications!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the main concepts but need more practice with torque calculations.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on the differences between static, dynamic, and rotational equilibrium.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basic equilibrium conditions: $\Sigma F = 0$ for no acceleration, $\Sigma \tau = 0$ for no rotation.
